intro_band.agent_remit_summary: yaah is a local developer CLI that generates coding-agent configuration for Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot CLI from a single source of truth, and ships a built-in security toolset that runs against the configured agent's tool activity. The remit's central promise is that every generated agent configuration carries the same protective hooks — a linter, a command guard, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger — plus a built-in yaah serve MCP server and a per-session audit log. Authorized counterparties are the local developer, the configured agent's LLM provider, the context7 and pulumi MCP servers (the latter remote over TLS), the built-in yaah MCP server, and the coding-agent plugin marketplace. Catastrophic shell commands must be blocked before they execute; hardcoded credentials must be detected before edits are accepted; session-loaded files must not be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Go 1.25 CLI built on spf13/cobra, organised around the Interface to Registry to Generator pattern (described in AGENTS.md). The hook handlers live in pkg/hooks/handlers/ — guard.go (deterministic regex blocklist on Bash), secretscan.go (13-pattern scanner on Edit/Write/MultiEdit), sessionlog.go, linter.go, commentcheck.go. pkg/harness/harness.go is the orchestrator; pkg/session/store.go writes a structured per-session JSON record (tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, findings) under .claude/sessions/. The built-in yaah serve MCP server in pkg/mcpserver/ exposes seven tools with descriptions that describe the tool's function only (no instruction-like language). Generators in pkg/generator/ (claude.go, opencode.go, codex.go, copilot.go) emit per-agent native config, sharing the cross-agent event map in hookmap.go. Root .mcp.json and .claude/settings.json configure three MCP servers — context7 (npx -y @context7/mcp), pulumi (HTTP at https://mcp.ai.pulumi.com/mcp), and the built-in yaah server. AGENTS.md is auto-managed (with CLAUDE.md and GEMINI.md as symlinks) and session-loaded. go.mod and go.sum pin direct and transitive dependencies with hashes; .github/workflows/ci.yaml pins action SHAs and uses cosign + Syft SBOM at release.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy-implementation divergence in the generator layer: the remit asserts that every generated agent configuration carries the same protective hooks, but pkg/generator/hookmap.go leaves the Codex field blank for HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse, and pkg/generator/codex.go's GenerateHooks continues past any event whose target name is empty — so yaah generate --agent codex ships a .codex/hooks.json with no command guard and no secret scanner. pkg/generator/codex_test.go documents this in a code comment (// PreToolUse is not supported by Codex.) and asserts the resulting data is nil, confirming the divergence is known to the code and unaddressed in the spec.

Layered on top: the context7 MCP server is launched via unpinned npx -y @context7/mcp (a silent-update vector in direct contradiction of the "third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned" remit clause); MCP tool calls fall outside PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks entirely, so the command guard, secret scanner, and session log never see them; the auto-managed AGENTS.md (with CLAUDE.md/GEMINI.md symlinks) is session-loaded and writable; and there are no *_test.go files for guard.go, secretscan.go, or sessionlog.go, so the controls the remit treats as load-bearing have no adversarial test fixture asserting they actually catch what they claim to catch.

rules.rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger.

Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute.
Remote MCP servers MUST be reached over TLS.

MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.
Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.
Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.
High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.
The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.
The MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files MUST stay consistent with each other.
A dangerous shell command caught by the command guard is blocked, not run, and the block is recorded to the session audit log.

The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.
Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.
Dependencies MUST be version-controlled with a committed, pinned lockfile, and the dependency tree kept small and reviewable.

It does not send email, post to external services, or make outbound network calls beyond the configured agent's LLM provider, the configured MCP servers, the package registry used to install third-party MCP servers, and the coding-agent plugin marketplace.
The harness's central promise is that its protections are uniform across every coding agent it generates configuration for.

findings.summary: Generated Codex CLI config ships with no PreToolUse or PostToolUse hooks — the command guard and secret scanner advertised as "uniform across every coding agent" are silently absent from `yaah generate --agent codex` output.
findings.description: pkg/generator/hookmap.go defines a cross-agent event map where each event names its equivalent for Claude, OpenCode, Codex, and Copilot — but the HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse entries leave the Codex field blank. CodexEventName(event) therefore returns the empty string for both events, and pkg/generator/codex.go's GenerateHooks continues past any event whose Codex name is empty (the early if codexEvent == "" || codexEvent == "notify" filter). The generated .codex/hooks.json ends up with SessionStart, Stop, and Notification (via config.toml) but no command guard and no secret scanner. pkg/generator/codex_test.go's TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported actively asserts this — its inline comment reads "PreToolUse is not supported by Codex" and the test confirms data == nil. This is the canonical case the remit's "protections are uniform" clause is meant to prevent.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every generated agent configuration MUST carry the same set of protective hooks — a linter, a command guard that blocks catastrophic shell commands, a secret scanner, a comment checker, and a session logger. / The harness's central promise is that its protections are uniform across every coding agent it generates configuration for.

evidence.snippet: HookPreToolUse entry: `{Claude: "PreToolUse", OpenCode: "tool.execute.before", Copilot: "preToolUse"}` — the `Codex` field is omitted, so `CodexEventName(HookPreToolUse)` returns the empty string.
HookPostToolUse entry: same shape, no Codex value; the secret-scanner event is dropped on the Codex target by the same path.
GenerateHooks body: `if codexEvent == "" || codexEvent == "notify" { continue }` — any event without a Codex name is silently skipped during emission.

TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported documents the behavior in a code comment ("PreToolUse is not supported by Codex.") and asserts the generated hooks data is nil when only PreToolUse is registered, confirming the gap is known and intentional in the generator.

recommended_actions: Populate the Codex field in pkg/generator/hookmap.go for HookPreToolUse and HookPostToolUse (Codex CLI does support PreToolUse / PostToolUse events in its hooks.json), then update TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported to assert the hooks land in the generated config rather than asserting they are dropped.

If a specific Codex protocol limitation actually prevents this, change the remit so its "uniform across every coding agent" promise honestly carves out Codex, and surface the limitation in yaah generate --agent codex output so operators see what they are losing.

findings.summary: MCP tool invocations bypass the hook chain entirely — the command guard, secret scanner, and session log never see them on any target.
findings.description: The hook configuration in .claude/settings.json (and the corresponding generated configs) matches (?i)^Bash$ for PreToolUse and (?i)^(Edit|Write|MultiEdit)$ for PostToolUse. Those matchers are deliberately narrow — they only fire on the configured coding agent's built-in Bash/Edit/Write tools. When the agent calls a tool exposed by any of the configured MCP servers — context7, pulumi, or the built-in yaah server — the call does not match either matcher and the yaah hook dispatcher is never invoked. The remit names "destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute" together in the approval-required list; the harness covers the first two for Bash/Edit/Write but the MCP-tool path is uninstrumented, so a write/send/execute tool from a third-party MCP server runs ungated and unrecorded.
findings.policy_rule_text: Catastrophic shell commands — recursive deletion from the filesystem root, force-pushing to a main branch, hard resets, destructive database statements, filesystem formatting, raw disk writes — MUST be blocked before they execute. / Hardcoded credentials in files the agent edits MUST be detected before the change is accepted.

evidence.snippet: PreToolUse matcher: "(?i)^Bash$" — only the literal `Bash` tool name fires the command guard; MCP tool names (e.g. mcp__yaah__yaah_check_command, mcp__pulumi__*, mcp__context7__*) never match.
PostToolUse matcher: "(?i)^(Edit|Write|MultiEdit)$" — same shape; MCP tools that write or delete are not seen by the secret scanner.

bashMatch = regexp.MustCompile("(?i)^Bash$") — the guard handler's own Match() is hard-coded to the Bash tool name, so even if the matcher were widened in settings, the handler would still skip non-Bash tool names.

recommended_actions: Add a hook rule that fires for the mcp__* tool-name family (the conventional Claude Code naming for MCP tools) and dispatch the call through a policy that classifies the MCP tool by its declared capability (read vs write/send/execute); block or queue for approval anything in the latter set.

At minimum, record every MCP tool call in the session log (pkg/session/store.go) so the audit trail covers more than just Bash/Edit/Write — currently a write performed via the pulumi MCP server leaves no record in .claude/sessions/<id>.json.

findings.summary: File writes and MCP write/send/execute tools run without a human approval gate — the remit's "MUST reach a human checkpoint" clause is not enforced in code.
findings.description: The remit's Approval Requirements section requires that destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write/send/execute reach a human checkpoint before they run. In the harness, only the catastrophic subset of Bash commands is gated (the CommandGuard blocks outright rather than prompting); every Edit/Write/MultiEdit operation that does not contain a regex-detected secret is passed through without any approval step, and MCP tool calls are not in the dispatch path at all (PRAX-2026-05-25-002). There is no PermissionRequest hook implementation, no Permissions schema in pkg/schema/, and no requireApproval field in any of the hook handlers — the only enforcement primitive in pkg/hooks/handlers/ is Block: true, which is binary.
findings.policy_rule_text: High-impact actions — destructive shell commands, file writes, and MCP tools that write, send, or execute — MUST reach a human checkpoint before they run.

evidence.snippet: Execute returns &hooks.Result{Block: true} on a regex match — binary block/allow only, no approval-queue concept.
Execute either blocks (on a secret hit) or returns nil; non-blocking edits proceed without operator confirmation.
HookType constants are command, http, prompt, agent — there is no approval-gate type, and no handler in pkg/hooks/handlers/ invokes Claude Code's PermissionRequest flow.

recommended_actions: Add a RequireApproval handler that returns a structured Result indicating the call should be paused for operator confirmation (Claude Code supports an approval prompt via the PermissionRequest event); wire it for Edit/Write/MultiEdit and for any MCP tool tagged write/send/exec.
Document in pkg/hooks/handlers/doc.go which call sites trigger approval and which do not, so the remit's promise and the implementation reality stay in sync.

findings.summary: `context7` MCP server installs unpinned via `npx -y @context7/mcp` — a silent-update vector and a direct contradiction of the "third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned" remit clause.
findings.description: Both the root .mcp.json and pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go configure the context7 MCP server as command: "npx", args: ["-y", "@context7/mcp"]. With no version pin, npx -y fetches the latest published version of @context7/mcp on every cold run; a compromised release would land in the agent's tool surface with no review and no integrity check. The harness has no equivalent of go.sum's cryptographic hash for its third-party MCP servers, and the doctor command only checks whether npx is on the PATH — not what version of @context7/mcp would be fetched. The other two configured servers do not have this problem (the built-in yaah server is a local binary; pulumi is a remote HTTPS endpoint), but context7 is exactly the kind of "auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run" pattern the remit's Known Good Baseline forbids.
findings.policy_rule_text: Third-party MCP servers MUST be pinned to a known-good, integrity-checked version — no server package auto-installed afresh, unpinned, on every run.

evidence.snippet: "context7": { "type": "stdio", "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "@context7/mcp"] } — no @x.y.z version specifier on the package name.
same shape duplicated in the mcpServers block of the Claude settings file (the file maintains two copies; see PRAX-2026-05-25-009).
Context7.Server() returns the same unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp` definition, so even regenerating the config from the harness does not pin it.

recommended_actions: Pin the package version in pkg/mcp/providers/context7.go: Args: []string{"-y", "@context7/mcp@<exact-version>"} — and consider --package=@context7/mcp@<ver> with an integrity-pinned lock file at the npm layer.
Add a yaah doctor check that warns when any registered MCP provider's Command + Args includes npx -y without a @version pin, so future additions cannot regress past the remit.

findings.summary: No adversarial test suite, no `SECURITY.md`, no threat model — the remit's "SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections" clause is unkept.
findings.description: The remit's Escalation and Limits section names four specific adversarial-test scenarios the project should run: secret-scanner planted-secret recovery, command-guard catastrophic-command block, poisoning-write prevention on a session-loaded file, and protection-uniformity verification on every generated config. There are no *_test.go files for pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go, pkg/hooks/handlers/secretscan.go, or pkg/hooks/handlers/sessionlog.go — the three security-critical handlers. There is no SECURITY.md at the repo root, no THREAT_MODEL.md, no docs/security.md, and no documented security-disclosure process. The unit tests that do exist cover generators (codex_test.go, opencode_test.go, copilot_test.go, settings_test.go) and registries — none assert that a planted OpenAI-style key literal trips SecretScanner.ScanFile, none assert that rm -rf / is blocked by CommandGuard.CheckCommand, and none assert PRAX-2026-05-25-001's gap (Codex hooks present) would actually fail today's test suite.
findings.policy_rule_text: The project SHOULD publish a threat model and a security-disclosure process, and SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — confirming that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write into a session-loaded file is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims.

evidence.snippet: workspace listing — files present: commentcheck.go, doc.go, guard.go, linter.go, secretscan.go, sessionlog.go — zero *_test.go files.
workspace root — no SECURITY.md, no THREAT_MODEL.md, no SECURITY_DISCLOSURE.md.
TestCodex_GenerateHooks_NoSupported actively documents the PRAX-2026-05-25-001 gap as expected behavior, which is the opposite of an adversarial test ensuring the protections are uniform.

recommended_actions: Add guard_test.go, secretscan_test.go, and a bootstrap_guard_test.go (once PRAX-2026-05-25-007's handler exists) with the four remit-named scenarios as table-driven test cases — and gate CI on them.
Author a top-level SECURITY.md covering the threat model (who is the attacker, what is in scope, what is explicitly out of scope) and the disclosure process; link it from the README.

findings.summary: Session audit log captures Bash/Edit/Write tool calls but not MCP tool calls — the "durable record of tool calls" promise has a known blind spot.
findings.description: pkg/session/store.go and pkg/session/session.go define a structured Session JSON document with ToolCalls, BlockedCalls, FilesModified, and Findings arrays — written atomically (temp file + rename) under .claude/sessions/<id>.json. pkg/harness/harness.go's HandleHookEvent records every tool call that reaches it. The blind spot is upstream: only the events whose matchers fire (Bash/Edit/Write/MultiEdit on the configured agent's built-in tools, plus session lifecycle events) reach HandleHookEvent at all, so MCP tool calls — the same set named in R-07 as approval-required — are absent from the resulting record. An operator inspecting yaah session show <id> after an incident involving an MCP tool call would see no trace of it.
findings.policy_rule_text: The harness MUST maintain a durable, structured, timestamped record of tool calls, blocked actions, and file modifications — detailed enough to reconstruct what the agent did in a session.

evidence.snippet: Save writes the session to .claude/sessions/<id>.json atomically with temp-file + rename — confirms the durable, structured part of the promise.
Session struct carries ToolCalls, BlockedCalls, FilesModified, Findings — the data model is present.

HandleHookEvent only records a ToolCallRecord for events that the matcher chain accepted; MCP tool invocations never reach this code path because the generated configs do not wire a hook rule for the mcp__* tool family (see PRAX-2026-05-25-002).

recommended_actions: Add an MCP tool-call hook (see PRAX-2026-05-25-002 fix); the same dispatch will produce a ToolCallRecord and close the gap automatically.
Or, if the deliberate scope is Bash/Edit/Write only, rewrite the remit's "record of tool calls" clause to name which tool calls and document the exclusion.

findings.summary: Third-party MCP tool descriptions for `context7` and `pulumi` are never inspected — the remit's "MUST NOT contain instruction-like language" rule cannot be enforced for them.
findings.description: The remit requires that MCP tool descriptions not contain instruction-like language directed at the model — both the built-in server's descriptions and, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures. The built-in yaah serve server's seven tool descriptions in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go are clean (functional descriptions only — "Scan a file for hardcoded secrets and credentials", "Check whether a shell command is safe to run", and so on). But there is no code path that fetches the tools/list response from context7 or pulumi after they are registered to validate their tool descriptions against the no-instruction-language rule — no yaah doctor check, no yaah mcp scan subcommand, no integration with pkg/mcpserver/'s capabilities. The remit's "as far as the harness can inspect them" qualifier leaves room here, but the harness is not currently doing the inspection.

tags.label: Tool descriptions contain no hidden model instructions

findings.policy_rule_text: MCP tool descriptions MUST NOT contain instruction-like language directed at the model — neither the built-in server's tool descriptions nor, as far as the harness can inspect them, those of the third-party servers it configures.

evidence.snippet: yaah's own seven tool descriptions are functional and clean (e.g. "Scan a file for hardcoded secrets and credentials") — the built-in half of R-04 is satisfied.
provider only carries Name/Command/Args — no field for capturing or validating the server's advertised tool descriptions after launch.
yaah doctor checks MCP servers for binary presence on the PATH only; there is no equivalent check that fetches tools/list from each server and scans the descriptions.

recommended_actions: Add a yaah mcp scan (or extend yaah doctor) that, for every registered MCP provider, performs a one-shot stdio/HTTP handshake, fetches tools/list, and runs a description-language linter (looks for second-person pronouns, imperatives, "When using this tool you should ...", etc.) — surface any hits as findings recorded to the session log.

findings.summary: Auto-managed session-loaded `AGENTS.md` (with `CLAUDE.md`/`GEMINI.md` symlinks) is writable through the same Edit/Write path that any other file is — no integrity guard on the bootstrap surface.
findings.description: The workspace's AGENTS.md opens with <!-- Managed by agent: keep sections and order; edit content, not structure --> and is divided into agent-regenerable sections (<!-- AGENTS-GENERATED:START commands -->, filemap, golden-samples, repo-settings, …). CLAUDE.md and GEMINI.md are symlinks to it, so the same file is session-loaded by Claude, Codex, and Gemini agents. The PostToolUse secret scanner only flags secrets in edited files — it does not flag writes into a session-loaded bootstrap file, and there is no separate handler that enforces "an edit to AGENTS.md must be operator-reviewed before it lands in the next session's context." The remit's R-06 explicitly forbids this shape: a write into a session-loaded file from agent-ingested content silently re-enters context on the next run.
findings.policy_rule_text: Agent-managed, session-loaded files MUST NOT be writable in a way that lets ingested content persist into future sessions unreviewed.

evidence.snippet: <!-- Managed by agent: keep sections and order; edit content, not structure --> — file declares itself agent-writable.
<!-- AGENTS-GENERATED:START commands --> … several auto-generated blocks; the file is designed to be re-emitted by the agent.
workspace listing — CLAUDE.md -> AGENTS.md and GEMINI.md -> AGENTS.md symlinks ensure a single edit reaches every agent's bootstrap surface.
PostToolUse matcher is (?i)^(Edit|Write|MultiEdit)$ and the handler scans only for credential patterns — there is no separate handler that guards the integrity of AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md / GEMINI.md.

recommended_actions: Add a BootstrapGuard PostToolUse handler that flags any Edit/Write/MultiEdit whose file_path resolves to a session-loaded file (start with AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, and the <!-- AGENTS-GENERATED:* --> block boundaries) and routes it through the approval flow proposed in PRAX-2026-05-25-003.
Track and commit changes to AGENTS.md separately from code changes so the bootstrap-surface diff is reviewable in version control.

findings.summary: The MCP server list is duplicated between `.mcp.json` and `.claude/settings.json`'s `mcpServers` block — a drift risk the remit explicitly names.
findings.description: Both .mcp.json (the Claude Code project-level MCP config) and the mcpServers block in .claude/settings.json declare the same three servers (context7, pulumi, yaah) with identical shapes. The remit's R-09 requires the MCP server configuration and the per-agent configuration files to stay consistent — but the harness ships two copies of the same information rather than one, so a future edit that updates one without the other will silently drift. Today the two files match; tomorrow's manual edit to one of them is a divergence waiting to happen.

evidence.snippet: top-level mcpServers block defining context7, pulumi, yaah.
duplicate mcpServers block, same three servers, byte-identical entries — so today's state is consistent but the duplication is a drift surface.

recommended_actions: Either generate one of the two files from the other (e.g. make .claude/settings.json's mcpServers block a symlink-equivalent merge of .mcp.json), or have the yaah generate flow refuse to emit when the two would diverge.

positives.title: Deterministic command guard with explicit catastrophic-command blocklist
positives.description: pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go runs on every PreToolUse Bash event and blocks `rm -rf /`, force-push to main/master, `git reset --hard`, `DROP TABLE`, system-file truncation, `mkfs`, and `dd of=/dev/*` — deterministic regex, no LLM in the loop, returns Block: true and the matched rule's reason.

positives.title: 13-pattern secret scanner wired into every Edit/Write/MultiEdit
positives.description: pkg/hooks/handlers/secretscan.go ships with patterns for AWS credentials, GitHub PATs/OAuth tokens, OpenAI keys, Anthropic keys, generic password literals, bearer tokens, private keys, Slack tokens/webhooks, and SendGrid keys; runs file-by-file, blocks the edit on a hit with a remediation message.

positives.title: Durable structured per-session audit log
positives.description: pkg/session/store.go writes a Session JSON document per session under .claude/sessions/<id>.json via temp-file + rename — atomic, structured, with timestamped ToolCalls, BlockedCalls, FilesModified, and Findings arrays; `yaah session list/show/clean` exposes the operator-facing surface.

positives.title: Exact-pinned Go dependency graph with cryptographic integrity
positives.description: go.mod pins direct deps to exact versions (go-sdk v1.4.0, cobra v1.10.2), and go.sum carries h1: hashes for every transitive module — small tree (eight transitive modules) — so dependency-confusion and silent-upgrade attacks at the Go layer are not feasible.

positives.title: Built-in MCP server tool descriptions are clean — no instruction-like language
positives.description: All seven tools in pkg/mcpserver/tools.go (yaah_scan_secrets, yaah_lint, yaah_check_command, yaah_doctor, yaah_session_info, yaah_planning_status, yaah_planning_init) carry functional descriptions only ("Scan a file for hardcoded secrets and credentials", "Check whether a shell command is safe to run", …) — no second-person imperatives, no embedded model instructions, satisfies the built-in half of remit R-04.

positives.title: CI pins GitHub Action SHAs and releases ship with cosign + Syft SBOM
positives.description: .github/workflows/ci.yaml references every action by full commit SHA with a version comment, and the release job uses sigstore/cosign-installer and anchore/sbom-action/download-syft before invoking GoReleaser — meaningful supply-chain hygiene at the build layer.

rows.content_type: plaintext, append-only, RFC3339-stamped session lifecycle events
rows.purpose: SessionStart/SessionEnd/Stop event trail with session id and cwd

rows.content_type: structured JSON, one file per session id, written atomically
rows.purpose: full per-session audit record — tool calls (with timestamp/name/input summary), blocked calls (with reason), files modified, structured findings

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. yaah ships real, operative defenses on the Claude path — a deterministic command guard, a 13-pattern secret scanner, a durable per-session JSON audit log, exact-pinned Go deps with go.sum hashes, and a built-in MCP server whose tool descriptions are clean — so Supply Chain and Monitor Continuously land at Established and Zero Trust and Balance Your Knowledge Base lift to Partial. What keeps the overall in the Partial band rather than higher is that the protections do not actually cover every target the harness generates for (Codex CLI gets no PreToolUse / PostToolUse hooks), MCP tool calls bypass the hook path on every target, the project's own adversarial-test promise is unkept (no unit tests for guard/secretscan/sessionlog, no SECURITY.md, no threat model), and one declared third-party MCP server installs unpinned via `npx -y`.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The CLI's tool surface is narrow and explicit — six cobra subcommands (`generate`, `hook`, `info`, `version`, `doctor`, `serve`, `session`, `skills`) with no general-purpose shell, no autonomous loop, and a remit that names the seven authorized built-in MCP tools (`yaah_scan_secrets`, `yaah_lint`, `yaah_check_command`, `yaah_doctor`, `yaah_session_info`, `yaah_planning_status`, `yaah_planning_init`) — all but one are read-only.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The secret scanner in pkg/hooks/handlers/secretscan.go runs on every Edit/Write/MultiEdit and the command guard in pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go runs on every Bash, so untrusted content the agent writes is checked before it lands — but the auto-managed AGENTS.md is session-loaded and writable through the same Edit/Write path with no integrity check guarding the bootstrap surface, leaving a one-hop poisoning channel into every future session.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: A real deterministic command guard (regex blocklist for rm -rf /, force-push to main, git reset --hard, DROP TABLE, mkfs, raw disk writes, system-file truncation) is wired into PreToolUse and a 13-pattern secret scanner is wired into PostToolUse for Claude / OpenCode / Copilot — that earns Partial; but the same hooks are silently absent from generated Codex CLI configs (hookmap.go leaves Codex blank for PreToolUse/PostToolUse), MCP tool calls bypass the hook path entirely, and there is no human-in-the-loop approval gate on file writes or write/send/execute MCP tools, all of which the remit names as approval-required actions.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: go.mod pins two direct deps to exact versions and go.sum carries cryptographic hashes for the full transitive set; .github/workflows/ci.yaml pins every action by SHA with a version comment and the release path uses cosign + Syft SBOM; the dependency tree is small (eight transitive modules). The one operative gap is the context7 MCP server, configured via unpinned `npx -y @context7/mcp` in both .mcp.json and the registered Context7 provider — a silent-update vector inside an otherwise tight supply chain.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The remit explicitly states the project SHOULD run adversarial testing of its own protections — that the secret scanner catches a planted secret, the command guard blocks a catastrophic command, a poisoning write is prevented, and a generated configuration genuinely carries the protections it claims — but there is no *_test.go for pkg/hooks/handlers/guard.go, secretscan.go, or sessionlog.go, no SECURITY.md, no threat model, no adversarial fixture suite, and no documented disclosure process; the unit tests that do exist cover generators and registries, not the security controls.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: pkg/session/store.go writes a structured per-session JSON record with timestamps, tool calls, blocked calls, files modified, and a typed findings array under .claude/sessions/; pkg/hooks/handlers/sessionlog.go writes an append-only RFC3339-stamped session-event log to .claude/logs/sessions.log; `yaah session list / show` lets the operator reconstruct activity. The gap is that MCP tool calls invoked by the configured agent do not flow through HandleHookEvent and so never enter either log.
